results.possibleIdentity: Rosemarie Castoro (1939–2015) was an American artist, choreographer, and dancer who was a central figure in the Minimalist and Conceptualist art scenes in New York City. Born in Brooklyn and a graduate of the Pratt Institute (1963), she famously described herself as a "paintersculptor." Her work spanned multiple disciplines, including abstract painting, conceptual text pieces, concrete poetry, performance, and large-scale sculpture. In the mid-1960s, she gained recognition for her systematic "Y" and "Interference" paintings, which explored color and structural composition. By the late 1960s, she transitioned toward Conceptualism and Post-Minimalism, creating works like the "Inventory" series (1968–69) and "Crackings" (1969), which involved site-specific interventions such as applying silver tape to city sidewalks. Castoro was closely associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement; notably, she was a performer in Yvonne Rainer's "Carriage Discreteness" during the landmark "9 Evenings: Theatre & Engineering" event in 1966, which served as the catalyst for E.A.T. She also participated in an E.A.T. Benefit exhibition at the Castelli Gallery. Throughout the 1970s and beyond, her work became increasingly sculptural and architectural, exemplified by her "Brushstrokes" (1971) and the totemic "Flashers" (1979) series. She shared a SoHo loft with her then-husband Carl Andre, which became a social hub for prominent artists like Sol LeWitt and Lawrence Weiner. Despite her prolific output and involvement in the Art Workers' Coalition, her contributions were often overshadowed by her male contemporaries until a series of major posthumous retrospectives brought renewed institutional attention to her legacy.

results.possibleIdentity: Mary Ascher (1900–1988) was an American painter, printmaker, and author who was active in New York City. She is noted for her abstract works and her thematic exploration of biblical narratives and modern technological power. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, she produced a significant series of paintings titled "Corridors of Power" (1967–1970), which addressed societal and technological shifts. Works in this series, such as "Automation II" (1967) and "Finance and Industry II" (1967), utilized motifs like hands and machines to explore deindividuation and the impact of "instant communication" on the world. This focus on the intersection of art and technology aligns with the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), and her work was recently featured in the "Visible Communication" exhibition at Baruch College (2025) alongside technology-focused artists like Juan Downey and Andy Warhol. Ascher served as the president of the American Society of Contemporary Artists from 1973 to 1975 and held a 30-year retrospective at the National Arts Club in New York in 1973. Her publications include "Twelve Women of the New Testament and Early Church." Her papers are held by the Smithsonian Institution's Archives of American Art, and her work is included in the collection of the Smithsonian American Art Museum.

results.possibleIdentity: Marilyn Wood (1929–2016) was an American choreographer, dancer, and intermedia artist renowned as a "choreographer of cities." She was a founding member of the Merce Cunningham Dance Company, performing and touring with Cunningham, John Cage, and Robert Rauschenberg from 1958 to 1963. In 1968, she transitioned from formal dance to found "Marilyn Wood and the Celebration Group," a collaborative ensemble of dancers, visual artists, filmmakers, architects, and musicians. Her work specialized in large-scale, site-specific "Celebrations" designed to reclaim urban public spaces and foster community participation. Her most iconic work, "Celebrations in City Places: The Seagram Building" (1972), utilized the entire 44-story facade of the New York City skyscraper for a vertical performance. Wood's practice often intersected with the avant-garde and technological art movements of the late 1960s and early 1970s; she was associated with the same circles as Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), and her group was featured alongside E.A.T. in events like the 11th Annual Avant Garde Festival of New York (1974). Her "environmental scoring" and multi-disciplinary approach mirrored the collaborative ethos of E.A.T. She later founded the International Center for Celebration (ICC) in Santa Fe in 1987.

results.possibleIdentity: John Handel Evans (often cited as J. Evans or J. Handel Evans) was an artist and educator active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is specifically associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) through his contributions to the journal 'Leonardo', where he wrote about the intersection of art and science. In 1970, he published a letter in 'Leonardo' (Vol. 3, No. 4) discussing the legacy of light artist Thomas Wilfred (pioneer of Lumia), a subject closely aligned with the technological interests of E.A.T. Beyond his artistic practice, he transitioned into university administration, serving as a professor and later as the president of California State University, San Bernardino, and as the founding president of California State University, Channel Islands.

John Evans (1932–2012) was a prominent New York-based collage artist and a central figure in the 'Avenue B School of Art'. Beginning in 1964 and continuing for nearly forty years, Evans created a daily collage on pages of bound sketchbooks using 'flotsam and jetsam' collected from the streets of the East Village. He was active in the experimental art and mail art networks of the 1960s and 1970s, corresponding with artists such as On Kawara and Ray Johnson. His work is sometimes categorized alongside Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in archival collections due to his involvement in the collaborative and networking-based art movements of the era. His collages often functioned as 'mini-time capsules' of the social and political atmosphere of late 20th-century New York.
